heroText: Thimphu is a capital city in Bhutan, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

highlights.name: Thimphu
highlights.text: Thimphu is a capital city in Bhutan, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

history.text: In 1885, a battle was held at what is now the Changlimithang sports ground in Thimphu.
Reforms continued and in 1952 the decision was made to shift the capital from the ancient capital of Punakha to Thimphu.
The fourth king, who had established the National Assembly in 1953, devolved all executive powers to a council of ministers elected by the people in 1998.

Before 1960, Thimphu consisted of a group of hamlets scattered across the valley including Motithang, Changangkha, Changlimithang, Langchupakha, and Taba, some of which constitute districts of the city today (see below for district details).
In 1961, Thimphu officially became the capital of Bhutan.
Bhutan joined the Colombo Plan in 1962, the Universal Postal Union in 1969 and became a member of the United Nations in 1971.
The modern Changlimithang Stadium was built on the site in 1974.
The National Constitution Committee in Thimphu started drafting the Constitution of the Kingdom of Bhutan in 2001.
In 2005, the fourth king of Bhutan announced his decision to hand over the reins of his kingdom to his son Prince Jigme Khesar Namgyal Wangchuk.

In 2008, this paved way for the transition from absolute monarchic rule to a parliamentary democratic constitutional monarchy, with Thimphu as the headquarters of the new government, with the national defined objective of achieving.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: The decisive victory opened the way for Ugyen Wangchuck, the first King of Bhutan, to virtually control the whole country.

history.label: Today
history.text: Since this time the sports ground has been of major importance to the city; football, cricket matches and archery competitions take place there.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The fourth king, Jigme Singye Wangchuck, opened the country for development and India provided the needed impetus in this process with financial and other forms of assistance.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Punakha, the old capital of Bhutan, is on the windward side with broad-leaved trees dominating the topography.
